macro_segments.title: The Status of Parah Adumah and Eglah Arufah as Shechitah She'einah Re'uyah

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Shechting the Parah Adumah and Its Questionable Status as Proper Slaughter
The Parah Adumah's Dual Status: Korban or Bedek HaBayis; Can It Receive Tumah as Food?
Rabbi Shimon Lakish's Teaching: The Parah Adumah Can Be Redeemed Even After Slaughter, Thus Theoretically Edible
The Challenge: If the Parah Can Theoretically Become Edible Through Redemption, Why Is Shechitah Not Re'uyah?
Rabbi Yochanan's Solution: The Parah Adumah Does Not Belong in the Mishnah—Cross It Out
The Eglah Arufah Case: When Does It Become Forbidden in Benefit?
The Eglah Arufah Mishnah Challenge: If Status Is Fixed Before Neck-Breaking, Why Can't Shechitah Help?
Rabbi Yanai's Teaching: The Eglah Arufah's Status Becomes Fixed When Brought Down to the Nachal Eitan
Resolving the Contradiction: Different Stages—Before vs. After Descent to the Wadi
Clarification: The Tradition About Crossing Out Belongs to Reish Lakish, Not Rabbi Yanai
Rabbi Ashi's Question: Does Reish Lakish Really Hold That Status Is Never Fixed?
The Tzipporei Metzora Precedent: When Do Ritual Birds Become Forbidden in Benefit?
Reish Lakish's Gezeirah Shava: Taking a Calf and Taking Birds—Both Become Forbidden Before Final Act
The Core Issue Resolved: The Eglah Arufah Becomes Forbidden Upon Descent; Shechitah After That Point Fails

macro_segments.title: Regulatory and Ownership Issues in Oto Ve'Et Beno: Who Has the Right to Shecht First?

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: Two Buyers of Mother and Child—The First Buyer Has the Right to Shecht First
The Logic: The Seller Implicitly Permits the First Buyer to Shecht Immediately
If the Second Buyer Slaughters First: He Merits and Has Eaten Meat, but He Transgressed No Prohibition
The Gemara on Rav Yosef: This Is a Din, a Regulatory Matter—Not About Issur V'Heter
The Tosefta: The Second Buyer as Zariz—He Merits, Avoids Transgression, and Gains Meat

macro_segments.title: Order and Multiple Actors in Oto Ve'Et Beno: Does Order Matter? Must It Be the Same Person?

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: Slaughtering Mother Then Two Children—Eighty Lashes (Two Prohibitions); Child Then Mother—Uncertain Forty or Eighty
Mishnah Continuation: Grandmother, Grandchild, Then Middle Generation (Mother)—Uncertain Eighty Lashes
Sumchus in the Name of Rabbi Meir: Even in the Same Act, Two Prohibitions Yield Two Lashes
The Gemara's Question: Why Does the Torah Say 'You [Plural] Shall Not Slaughter' Instead of the Singular?
First Interpretation: The Plural Indicates Two Orders (Mother-Child and Child-Mother) Are Both Prohibited
Second Implication: The Plural Suggests Two Different Actors Can Transgress—Not Just One Person
Gemara's Refinement: The Plural's Primary Function Is to Include Two People, Not Merely Two Orders
Alternative: If Passive Voice Were Used ('They Shall Not Be Slaughtered'), Order Distinction Would Be Clearer

macro_segments.title: The Question of Lashes When the Same Prohibition Is Done Multiple Times in One Act

micro_segments.title: Abaye's Question to Rav Yosef: What Is Sumchus's Reason for Awarding Two Lashes?
First Possibility: Sumchus Holds That Even the Same Prohibition Doubled Without Interruption Yields Two Lashes or Sacrifices
The Gemara's Objection: If That Were True, Sumchus Should Have Said So in the First Mishnah Case
Alternative Explanation: The Rabbis Exempt the First Case Because Conceptually It Is One Thing Done Twice (Mother of A and Mother of B)
Proof from Kilayim Mishnah: The Doubling Language Suggests Two Sets of Lashes, But Requires Sequential Acts with Warning
The Gemara's Counter: The Kilayim Mishnah Is Not About Sequential Acts; It Teaches That There Are Two Types of Kilayim
Proof from Gid HaNasheh: Eating Two Kezaytim from Both Sinews—The Rabbis Say Two Lashes; Rabbi Yehuda Says One
The Case Must Be Simultaneous: Rabbi Yehuda's Doubtful Warning Theory Only Works If Both Kezaytim Are Eaten at Once
Resolution: The Rabbis' Two Lashes in This Case Show That Even the Same Prohibition Doubled Can Yield Two Lashes
Final Counter: Sequential Acts with Warning Between Them Explain the Two Lashes; No Proof for the Extreme View
The Gid Case with Rabbi Yehuda: Even a Doubtful Warning May Count as a Warning, Allowing Two Lashes if Acts Are Sequential

topical_tags.term: Kedushat Mizbe'ach vs. Kedushat Bedek HaBayis
Mishnah Cases: Mother-Child, Two Children-Mother, Grandmother-Grandchild-Mother
Sumchus in the name of Rabbi Meir
